questions.question: Explain the concept of exception handling in C++ and its advantages over traditional error handling.
keyPoints: Exception handling separates error detection from error handling
Uses try-catch-throw mechanism
More readable and maintainable than return codes
Allows centralized error handling
Provides structured way to handle runtime errors

questions.question: What is the difference between throw and rethrow in C++ exception handling?
keyPoints: throw creates and throws a new exception object
rethrow (throw;) propagates existing caught exception
throw can throw any type by value
rethrow rethrows the exact same exception that was caught
Used in different contexts - initial throw vs propagation

questions.question: Explain the different levels of exception safety with examples.
keyPoints: Basic guarantee: no leaks, object remains in valid state
Strong guarantee: operation succeeds or has no effect (transaction semantics)
No-throw guarantee: operation will never throw (destructors, swap)
Example: vector push_back provides strong guarantee
Destructors should provide no-throw guarantee

questions.question: What is the purpose of the catch(...) block? When and why should it be used?
keyPoints: Catches any type of exception regardless of type
Used as a catch-all handler
Useful for cleanup operations before program termination
Should be last catch block (most general)
Cannot access exception object but can rethrow

questions.question: Explain the concept of stack unwinding in C++ with an example.
keyPoints: When exception thrown, call stack is searched for handler
Local objects in each scope are destroyed (destructors called)
Process continues until matching catch found
If no handler found, terminate() is called
Ensures proper cleanup of resources (RAII)

questions.question: What are standard exception classes in C++? Mention at least 5 with their usage.
keyPoints: std::exception - base class for all
std::bad_alloc - new failure
std::runtime_error - general runtime errors
std::logic_error - logic errors (out_of_range, invalid_argument)
std::overflow_error - arithmetic overflow
All derive from std::exception

questions.question: Explain the noexcept specifier in C++11 with examples.
keyPoints: Declares function will not throw exceptions
If noexcept function throws, std::terminate is called
Used for optimization hints to compiler
Destructors should be noexcept by default
Can take boolean argument: noexcept(false) allows throwing

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating creation of custom exception class derived from std::exception. Include proper exception handling with multiple catch blocks showing polymorphism.
keyPoints: Define custom exception class inheriting from std::exception
Override what() method to return error message
Create try-catch blocks catching by reference
Demonstrate base class catch catching derived exceptions
Show proper exception propagation
Code should compile and demonstrate output

questions.question: Design a robust exception handling mechanism for a Banking application that handles operations like deposit, withdrawal, and balance inquiry. Include validation and custom exceptions.
keyPoints: Create custom exceptions: InsufficientFunds, InvalidAmount, AccountLocked
Implement try-catch for each operation
Show proper error messages and recovery
Use nested try-catch for complex operations
Demonstrate rethrowing for upper-level handlers
Show proper resource management with exceptions

questions.question: Explain exception handling best practices in C++. Discuss do's and don'ts with examples.
keyPoints: Do: Throw by value, catch by reference
Do: Catch by reference to avoid slicing
Do: Use specific catch blocks before general
Don't: Catch by value (slicing problem)
Don't: Throw raw pointers (use smart pointers)
Don't: Catch(...) without rethrow for cleanup only
Use RAII for resource management
Prefer standard exceptions for standard errors

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program demonstrating stack unwinding with multiple function calls and proper cleanup of resources using RAII principles.
keyPoints: Create functions at different call levels with try-throw
Show local objects being destroyed during unwinding
Implement RAII class (wrapper) for resource
Demonstrate destructor calls during unwinding
Show exception caught at appropriate level
Verify proper cleanup occurs
